500 Lab 4

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

for PQHS/CRSP 500

Author

Lily Zhang

Published

2026-03-23

R Setup

Code 
knitr::opts_chunk$set(comment = NA) 

library(janitor) 
library(naniar)
library(conflicted)
library(tableone)
library(gt)
library(mosaic)
library(broom)
library(Epi)
library(mice)
library(Matching)
library(survival)
library(cobalt)
library(rbounds)
library(twang)
library(survey)
library(easystats)
library(tidyverse) 
conflicts_prefer(janitor::clean_names, dplyr::select, dplyr::filter, 
                 base::mean, base::max, stats::var)
theme_set(theme_bw())

Loading the Data

Code 
url_1 <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/THOMASELOVE/500-data/master/data/dig1.csv"

dig1_raw <- read_csv(url_1, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(subjectid = as.character(subjectid)) |>
  clean_names()

dim(dig1_raw)
[1] 6800   72

I have 6800 rows and 72 columns here.

The variables in the raw data are listed.

Code 
names(dig1_raw)
 [1] "subjectid" "trtmt"     "age"       "race"      "sex"       "ejf_per"  
 [7] "ejfmeth"   "chestx"    "bmi"       "klevel"    "creat"     "digdoser" 
[13] "chfdur"    "rales"     "elevjvp"   "pedema"    "restdys"   "exertdys" 
[19] "actlimit"  "s3"        "pulcong"   "nsym"      "heartrte"  "diabp"    
[25] "sysbp"     "functcls"  "chfetiol"  "prevmi"    "angina"    "diabetes" 
[31] "hyperten"  "diguse"    "diuretk"   "diuret"    "ksupp"     "aceinhib" 
[37] "nitrates"  "hydral"    "vasod"     "digdose"   "cvd"       "cvddays"  
[43] "whf"       "whfdays"   "dig"       "digdays"   "mi"        "midays"   
[49] "uang"      "uangdays"  "strk"      "strkdays"  "sva"       "svadays"  
[55] "vena"      "venadays"  "crev"      "crevdays"  "ocvd"      "ocvddays" 
[61] "rinf"      "rinfdays"  "oth"       "othdays"   "hosp"      "hospdays" 
[67] "nhosp"     "death"     "deathday"  "reason"    "dwhf"      "dwhfdays"

1 Task 1. Create a Table 1

The primary outcome of this study is worsening heart failure (whf). The main exposure of interest is whether the patient used DIG within one week prior to randomization (diguse), coded as a binary indicator. All variables included in the analysis are summarized below.

Variable Description
whf Worsening heart failure (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
diguse Whether the patient used DIG within one week prior to randomization (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
age Age in years
race White (1) or Non-White (2)
sex Male (1) or Female (2)
bmi Body-Mass Index (\(kg/meters^2\))
ejf_per Ejection Fraction (percent)
creat Serum Creatinine level (mg/dl)
klevel Serum Potassium level (mEq/l)
sysbp Sysolic BP (mm Hg)
diabp Diastolic BP (mm Hg)
heartrte Heart Rate (beats/min)
diabetes History of Diabetes (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
hyperten History of Hypertension (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
prevmi Previous Myocardial Infarction (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
angina Current Angina (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
functcls Current NYHA Functional Class (1 = I, 2 = II, 3 = III, 4 = IV)

1.1 Selecting Variables

First, I will select only those variables I will actually use in our study, which includes the subject ID, the PREVMI information, and the 12 variables listed above.

Code 
dig <- dig1_raw |> 
  select(subjectid, whf, diguse, age, race, sex, bmi, 
         ejf_per, klevel, creat, sysbp, diabp, heartrte, 
         diabetes, hyperten, prevmi, angina, functcls, 
         chfdur, rales, elevjvp, pedema, restdys)

dim(dig)
[1] 6800   23

I now have 6800 observations on 18 variables in the dig tibble.

1.2 Converting Categorical Variables to Factors

First, a new variable event was created as a factor derived from the outcome whf, with levels coded as “Yes” and “No”.

Code 
dig$event <- factor(dig$whf, levels = c(1, 0), 
                    labels = c("Yes", "No"))

The categorical variables I will want to treat using factors in R are:

Variable Description
diguse Whether the patient used DIG within one week prior to randomization (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
race White (1) or Non-White (2)
sex Male (1) or Female (2)
diabetes History of Diabetes (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
hyperten History of Hypertension (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
prevmi Previous Myocardial Infarction (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
angina Current Angina (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
functcls Current NYHA Functional Class (1 = I, 2 = II, 3 = III, 4 = IV)
chfdur Approximate duration of CHF (months)
rales Rales (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)
elevjvp Elevated jugular venous pressure (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)
pedema Peripheral edema (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)
restdys Dyspnea at rest or orthopnea (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)
Code 
dig_c <- dig |>
  mutate(diguse_f = fct_recode(factor(diguse), Yes = "1", No = "0"), 
         diguse_f = fct_relevel(diguse_f, "Yes"),
         race_f = fct_recode(factor(race), White = "1", "Non-white" = "2"),
         race_f = fct_relevel(race_f, "Non-white"),
         sex_f = fct_recode(factor(sex), M = "1", F = "2"),
         sex_f = fct_relevel(sex_f, "M"),
         diab_f = fct_recode(factor(diabetes), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         diab_f = fct_relevel(diab_f, "Yes"), 
         hyper_f = fct_recode(factor(hyperten), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         hyper_f = fct_relevel(hyper_f, "Yes"), 
         prevmi_f = fct_recode(factor(prevmi), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         prevmi_f = fct_relevel(prevmi_f, "Yes"), 
         angina_f = fct_recode(factor(angina), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         angina_f = fct_relevel(angina_f, "Yes"), 
         nyha_f = fct_recode(factor(functcls), I = "1", II = "2",
                             III = "3", IV = "4"), 
         rales_f = factor(rales), 
         elevjvp_f = factor(elevjvp), 
         pedema_f = factor(pedema), 
         restdys_f = factor(restdys)) |> 
  select(subjectid, whf, event, diguse, diguse_f, age, race, race_f, 
         sex, sex_f, bmi, ejf_per, klevel, creat, sysbp, diabp, heartrte, 
         diabetes, diab_f, hyperten, hyper_f, prevmi, prevmi_f, 
         angina, angina_f, functcls, nyha_f, chfdur, rales, rales_f, 
         elevjvp, elevjvp_f, pedema, pedema_f, restdys, restdys_f)

dim(dig_c)
[1] 6800   36

Now, the variables are properly formatted for analysis.

1.3 Numeric Summaries for Continuous Variables

Code 
dig_c |> 
  select(age, bmi, ejf_per, klevel, creat, sysbp, diabp, heartrte, chfdur) |> 
  summary()
      age             bmi           ejf_per          klevel       
 Min.   :21.00   Min.   :14.45   Min.   : 3.00   Min.   :  0.000

 Max.   :360.00  
 NA's   :14

Ranges of the variables look reasonable.

1.4 Data Set After Cleaning

Missingness is examined for all variables.

Code 
miss_var_summary(dig_c)
# A tibble: 36 × 3
   variable  n_miss pct_miss
   <chr>      <int>    <num>
 1 klevel       801  11.8   
 2 chfdur        14   0.206 
 3 heartrte       8   0.118 
 4 functcls       6   0.0882
 5 nyha_f         6   0.0882
 6 diabp          5   0.0735
 7 elevjvp        4   0.0588
 8 elevjvp_f      4   0.0588
 9 pedema         4   0.0588
10 pedema_f       4   0.0588
# ℹ 26 more rows
Code 
miss_case_table(dig_c) 
# A tibble: 7 × 3
  n_miss_in_case n_cases pct_cases
           <int>   <int>     <dbl>
1              0    5956   87.6   
2              1     827   12.2   
3              2      11    0.162 
4              3       2    0.0294
5              4       1    0.0147
6              7       1    0.0147
7              8       2    0.0294

I do not have any missing values on the outcome whf.

1.6 Resulting Table 1

Code 
table1 <- CreateTableOne(data = dig_c, 
          vars = c("whf", "age", "race_f", "sex_f", "bmi", 
                   "ejf_per", "klevel", "creat", "sysbp", 
                   "diabp", "heartrte", "chfdur", "diab_f", 
                   "hyper_f", "prevmi_f", "angina_f", "nyha_f", 
                   "rales_f", "elevjvp_f", "pedema_f","restdys_f"),
          strata = c("diguse_f"))
table1
                      Stratified by diguse_f
                       Yes            No             p      test
  n                      3017           3783                    
  whf (mean (SD))        0.37 (0.48)    0.25 (0.44)  <0.001     
  age (mean (SD))       63.34 (10.80)  63.60 (11.01)  0.335     
  race_f = White (%)     2546 (84.4)    3263 (86.3)   0.033     
  sex_f = F (%)           699 (23.2)     820 (21.7)   0.150     
  bmi (mean (SD))       27.16 (5.29)   27.08 (5.11)   0.512     
  ejf_per (mean (SD))   27.74 (8.84)   29.18 (8.81)  <0.001     
  klevel (mean (SD))     4.32 (0.52)    4.46 (7.44)   0.342     
  creat (mean (SD))      1.29 (0.37)    1.28 (0.37)   0.326     
  sysbp (mean (SD))    125.76 (20.11) 125.82 (19.80)  0.898     
  diabp (mean (SD))     74.87 (11.05)  74.93 (11.43)  0.805     
  heartrte (mean (SD))  78.64 (12.62)  78.85 (12.69)  0.487     
  chfdur (mean (SD))    29.68 (35.84)  30.54 (37.69)  0.342     
  diab_f = No (%)        2204 (73.1)    2663 (70.4)   0.017     
  hyper_f = No (%)       1637 (54.3)    2078 (54.9)   0.590     
  prevmi_f = No (%)      1042 (34.5)    1338 (35.4)   0.498     
  angina_f = No (%)      2243 (74.4)    2734 (72.3)   0.058     
  nyha_f (%)                                          0.013     
     I                    393 (13.0)     514 (13.6)             
     II                  1574 (52.2)    2090 (55.3)             
     III                  979 (32.5)    1102 (29.2)             
     IV                    70 ( 2.3)      72 ( 1.9)             
  rales_f (%)                                         0.709     
     0                    849 (28.1)    1111 (29.4)             
     1                    193 ( 6.4)     239 ( 6.3)             
     2                   1651 (54.7)    2040 (54.0)             
     3                    324 (10.7)     390 (10.3)             
  elevjvp_f (%)                                       0.055     
     0                   1383 (45.8)    1858 (49.2)             
     1                    177 ( 5.9)     213 ( 5.6)             
     2                   1201 (39.8)    1415 (37.4)             
     3                    256 ( 8.5)     293 ( 7.8)             
  pedema_f (%)                                        0.657     
     0                   1453 (48.2)    1815 (48.0)             
     1                    187 ( 6.2)     209 ( 5.5)             
     2                    957 (31.7)    1211 (32.0)             
     3                    420 (13.9)     544 (14.4)             
  restdys_f (%)                                       0.937     
     0                    890 (29.5)    1128 (29.8)             
     1                    205 ( 6.8)     262 ( 6.9)             
     2                   1452 (48.1)    1820 (48.2)             
     3                    470 (15.6)     569 (15.1)

There is not much difference between the two treatment groups across all covariates.

2 Task 2. Unadjusted Analysis.

An unadjusted model (m1) was constructed to evaluate the effect of treatment on the outcome death.

Code 
temp <- twoby2(table(dig_c$diguse_f, dig_c$event))
2 by 2 table analysis: 
------------------------------------------------------ 
Outcome   : Yes 
Comparing : Yes vs. No 

     Yes   No    P(Yes) 95% conf. interval
Yes 1126 1891    0.3732    0.3561   0.3906
No   964 2819    0.2548    0.2412   0.2690

                                   95% conf. interval
             Relative Risk: 1.4646    1.3636   1.5731
         Sample Odds Ratio: 1.7413    1.5695   1.9319
Conditional MLE Odds Ratio: 1.7412    1.5673   1.9345
    Probability difference: 0.1184    0.0962   0.1405

             Exact P-value: 0.0000 
        Asymptotic P-value: 0.0000 
------------------------------------------------------
Code 
unadj <- glm(whf ~ diguse, data=dig_c, family=binomial())

model_parameters(unadj, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.95) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(columns = -df_error, decimals = 3)
Parameter Coefficient SE CI CI_low CI_high z df_error p
(Intercept) 0.342 0.013 0.950 0.318 0.368 −28.760 Inf 0.000
diguse 1.741 0.092 0.950 1.570 1.932 10.464 Inf 0.000

Patients who used Digoxin within one week prior to randomization had 1.741 times higher odds of worsening heart failure compared to those not used (OR = 1.741, 95% CI: 1.570 - 1.932), suggesting a meaningful difference between the two groups.

3 Task 3. 1:1 Matching.

3.1 Single Imputation

There is some missingness in the data set. To appropriately handle missing data, single imputation is performed prior to fitting the propensity score model.

Code 
dig_i <- 
  mice(dig_c, m = 1, seed = 500, print = FALSE) |>
  complete() |>
  tibble()
Warning: Number of logged events: 14

3.2 PS Model

A propensity score model was fitted using logistic regression to estimate the probability of treatment assignment conditional on observed covariates.

Code 
psmodel <- glm(diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + ejf_per + klevel + 
                 creat + sysbp + diabp + heartrte + chfdur + 
                 diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + angina + functcls + 
                 rales + elevjvp + pedema + restdys, 
               family=binomial(), data=dig_i)

summary(psmodel)

Call:
glm(formula = diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + ejf_per + klevel + 
    creat + sysbp + diabp + heartrte + chfdur + diabetes + hyperten + 
    prevmi + angina + functcls + rales + elevjvp + pedema + restdys, 
    family = binomial(), data = dig_i)

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)  0.1579078  0.4355176   0.363  0.71692    
age         -0.0025156  0.0022507  -1.118  0.26370    
race         0.1246329  0.0695824   1.791  0.07327 .  
sex          0.1034273  0.0597792   1.730  0.08360 .  
bmi          0.0030609  0.0047311   0.647  0.51764    
ejf_per     -0.0176119  0.0028550  -6.169 6.89e-10 ***
klevel      -0.0314225  0.0490035  -0.641  0.52137    
creat        0.0840852  0.0665173   1.264  0.20619    
sysbp       -0.0001385  0.0012332  -0.112  0.91061    
diabp       -0.0003266  0.0021813  -0.150  0.88096    
heartrte    -0.0015419  0.0019469  -0.792  0.42837    
chfdur      -0.0007030  0.0006691  -1.051  0.29341    
diabetes    -0.1287508  0.0546867  -2.354  0.01856 *  
hyperten     0.0265789  0.0493697   0.538  0.59033    
prevmi       0.0429138  0.0516030   0.832  0.40563    
angina      -0.1105922  0.0556891  -1.986  0.04705 *  
functcls     0.0581811  0.0364114   1.598  0.11007    
rales       -0.0008442  0.0286152  -0.030  0.97646    
elevjvp      0.0737537  0.0267979   2.752  0.00592 ** 
pedema      -0.0375802  0.0232826  -1.614  0.10651    
restdys     -0.0002660  0.0258362  -0.010  0.99179    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 9340.3  on 6799  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 9253.1  on 6779  degrees of freedom
AIC: 9295.1

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 9

Having fit the model, the raw and linear propensity score values are saved to the data set.

Code 
dig_i$ps <- psmodel$fitted
dig_i$linps <- psmodel$linear.predictors

3.3 Comparing Propensity Score Distribution

A numeric summary of the PS is printed:

Code 
df_stats(ps + linps ~ diguse, data = dig_i) |>
  gt() |> fmt_number(columns = c(-n, -missing), decimals = 3) |> 
  opt_stylize(style = 2, color = "cyan")
response diguse min Q1 median Q3 max mean sd n missing
ps 0.000 0.000 0.403 0.437 0.475 0.640 0.438 0.056 3783 0
ps 1.000 0.289 0.414 0.449 0.487 0.638 0.451 0.053 3017 0
linps 0.000 −14.077 −0.394 −0.255 −0.100 0.575 −0.276 0.661 3783 0
linps 1.000 −0.899 −0.348 −0.206 −0.052 0.567 −0.201 0.217 3017 0

The distribution of PS across the two treatment groups are compared.

Code 
ggplot(dig_i, aes(x = diguse_f, y = ps, color = diguse_f)) + 
    geom_boxplot() +
    geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.5) + 
    guides(color = "none")
Code 
ggplot(dig_i, aes(x = linps, fill = diguse_f)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.3)

We can see a fair amount of overlap across the two treatment groups. I will use Rubin’s Rules to help assess the amount of overlap at this point, before any adjustments for the propensity score.

3.4 Rubin’s Rules

3.4.1 Rubin’s Rule 1

First, the absolute value of the standardized difference of the linear propensity score, comparing the intervention group to the control group, should be close to 0, ideally below 10%, and in any case less than 50%.

Code 
rubin1.unadj <- with(dig_i,
     abs(100*(mean(linps[diguse==1])-mean(linps[diguse==0]))/sd(linps)))
rubin1.unadj
[1] 14.63593

Rubin’s Rule 1 is barely satisfied.

3.4.2 Rubin’s Rule 2

Second, the ratio of the variance of the linear propensity score in the intervention group to the variance of the linear propensity score in the control group should be close to 1, ideally between 4/5 and 5/4, but certainly between 1/2 and 2.

Code 
rubin2.unadj <- with(dig_i,
  var(linps[diguse == 1]) / var(linps[diguse == 0]))

rubin2.unadj
[1] 0.1078099

Rubin’s Rule 2 is not satisfied, further adjustment of the propensity score is needed to achieve better covariate balance. Therefore, a 1:1 greedy matching procedure will be performed.

3.5 1:1 Matching

3.5.1 Matching without Replacement

To match on the linear propensity score:

Code 
X <- dig_i$linps 
Tr <- as.logical(dig_i$diguse)
match1 <- Match(Tr=Tr, X=X, M = 1, replace=FALSE, ties=FALSE)
summary(match1)

Estimate...  0 
SE.........  0 
T-stat.....  NaN 
p.val......  NA 

Original number of observations..............  6800 
Original number of treated obs...............  3017 
Matched number of observations...............  3017 
Matched number of observations  (unweighted).  3017

3.5.2 Assessing the Balance

Below I will assess the match balance from the 1:1 matching.

Code 
set.seed(500)
mb1 <- MatchBalance(diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + 
                    ejf_per + klevel + creat + sysbp + diabp + 
                    heartrte + chfdur + diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + 
                    angina + functcls + rales + elevjvp + pedema + 
                    restdys + ps + linps, 
                    data=dig_i, match.out = match1, nboots=500)

***** (V1) age *****

mean control..........     63.597            63.292

Before Matching Minimum p.value: < 2.22e-16 
Variable Name(s): ejf_per ps linps  Number(s): 5 21 22 

After Matching Minimum p.value: < 2.22e-16 
Variable Name(s): ps linps  Number(s): 21 22
Code 
covnames <- c("age", "race", "sex", "bmi", 
              "ejf_per", "klevel", "creat", "sysbp", "diabp", 
              "heartrte", "chfdur", "diabetes", "hyperten", "prevmi",
              "angina", "functcls", "rales", "elevjvp", "pedema", 
              "restdys", "ps", "linps")

pre.szd <- NULL; post.szd <- NULL
for(i in 1:length(covnames)) {
pre.szd[i] <- mb1$BeforeMatching[[i]]$sdiff.pooled
post.szd[i] <- mb1$AfterMatching[[i]]$sdiff.pooled
}
Code 
match_szd <- data.frame(covnames, pre.szd, post.szd)
print(match_szd, digits=3)
   covnames pre.szd post.szd
1       age  -2.354    0.451
2      race   5.273    1.096
3       sex   3.579    0.236
4       bmi   1.577   -0.645
5   ejf_per -16.221   -2.430
6    klevel  -6.576   -1.248
7     creat   2.392    0.338
8     sysbp  -0.385    0.217
9     diabp  -0.560   -0.852
10 heartrte  -1.691   -0.329
11   chfdur  -2.426   -0.614
12 diabetes  -5.906   -0.149
13 hyperten   1.347   -0.200
14   prevmi   1.743    0.627
15   angina  -4.708    1.518
16 functcls   6.856    0.758
17    rales   2.785    0.687
18  elevjvp   6.639    2.132
19   pedema  -1.311    0.343
20  restdys   1.216    0.649
21       ps  22.556    2.815
22    linps  15.325    2.772

Next, a Love Plot is used to describing standardized differences before and after matching

Code 
b <- bal.tab(match1, 
             diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + ejf_per + klevel + 
                 creat + sysbp + diabp + heartrte + chfdur + 
                 diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + angina + functcls + 
                 rales + elevjvp + pedema + restdys + ps + linps, 
             data=dig_i, stats = c("m", "v"), un = TRUE,
             quick = FALSE)
Code 
p <- love.plot(b, threshold = .1, size = 3,
               var.order = "unadjusted", stars = "raw",
               title = "Standardized Differences and 1:1 Matching")
p

Variance ratios before and after matching are also illustrated.

Code 
p <- love.plot(b, stat = "v",
               threshold = 1.25, size = 3,
               var.order = "unadjusted",
               title = "Variance Ratios and 1:1 Matching")
p + theme_bw()

We can see that the balance of all covariates has improved after adjustment.

3.5.3 Rubin’s Rules 1 and 2 for Matching

Create a new data frame, containing only the matched sample.

Code 
matches_1 <- factor(rep(match1$index.treated, 2))

dig.matches_1 <- cbind(matches_1, 
          dig_i[c(match1$index.control, match1$index.treated),])

Rubin’s Rules 1:

Code 
rubin1.m1 <- with(dig.matches_1, 
      abs(100*(mean(linps[diguse==1])-mean(linps[diguse==0]))/sd(linps)))                         
rubin1.m1
[1] 2.823234

Rubin’s Rules 2:

Code 
rubin2.m1 <- with(dig.matches_1, 
                  var(linps[diguse == 1]) / var(linps[diguse == 0]))
rubin2.m1
[1] 1.078015

Rubin’s Rules 1 and 2 are both satisfied.

3.6 Causal Effect Estimate

Code 
whf_adj_m1 <- clogit(whf ~ diguse + strata(matches_1), data = dig.matches_1)

summary(whf_adj_m1)
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(rep(1, 6034L), whf) ~ diguse + strata(matches_1), 
    data = dig.matches_1, method = "exact")

  n= 6034, number of events= 1931 

          coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z Pr(>|z|)    
diguse 0.49986   1.64848  0.05697 8.774   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

       exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95
diguse     1.648     0.6066     1.474     1.843

Concordance= 0.622  (se = 0.019 )
Likelihood ratio test= 79.4  on 1 df,   p=<2e-16
Wald test            = 76.98  on 1 df,   p=<2e-16
Score (logrank) test = 78.6  on 1 df,   p=<2e-16
Code 
tidy(whf_adj_m1, exponentiate = TRUE, 
     conf.int = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)
term estimate std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high
diguse 1.648 0.057 8.774 0.000 1.474 1.843

After 1:1 propensity score matching, patients who used Digoxin within one week prior to randomization had 1.648 times the odds of having worsening heart failure compared to those not used, with a 95% CI ranging from 1.474 to 1.843, suggesting that there are meaningful difference between two groups.

3.7 Sensitivity Analyses After Matching

A Rosenbaum sensitivity analysis was conducted to assess the robustness of the estimated treatment effect to potential unmeasured confounding.

Code 
pair_data <- dig.matches_1 |>
  group_by(matches_1) |>
  summarise(y_treat = whf[diguse == 1],  
            y_ctrl  = whf[diguse == 0], 
            .groups = "drop")

out.tab <- table(
  No_diguseazine = pair_data$y_ctrl,
  diguseazine = pair_data$y_treat)

out.tab
              diguseazine
No_diguseazine    0    1
             0 1396  816
             1  495  310
Code 
binarysens(x = out.tab["1", "0"], y = out.tab["0", "1"], 
           Gamma = 2, GammaInc = 0.05)

 Rosenbaum Sensitivity Test 
 
Unconfounded estimate ....  0 

 Gamma Lower bound Upper bound
  1.00           0     0.00000
  1.05           0     0.00000
  1.10           0     0.00000
  1.15           0     0.00000
  1.20           0     0.00000
  1.25           0     0.00000
  1.30           0     0.00001
  1.35           0     0.00019
  1.40           0     0.00179
  1.45           0     0.01103
  1.50           0     0.04558
  1.55           0     0.13345
  1.60           0     0.29086
  1.65           0     0.49600
  1.70           0     0.69615
  1.75           0     0.84621
  1.80           0     0.93470
  1.85           0     0.97662
  1.90           0     0.99288
  1.95           0     0.99814
  2.00           0     0.99958

 Note: Gamma is Odds of Differential Assignment To
 Treatment Due to Unobserved Factors

The treatment effect remained robust to unmeasured confounding up to \(\Gamma = 1.65\). Beyond this level, the observed effect could be explained away by potential hidden bias. This suggests that the results are moderately sensitive to unmeasured confounding.

4 Task 4. Propensity Weighting Analysis.

4.1 Propensity Score Weighting (ATT approach)

Code 
dig_df <- data.frame(dig_i)
Code 
ps_dig <- ps(diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + ejf_per + klevel + 
                 creat + sysbp + diabp + heartrte + chfdur + 
                 diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + angina + functcls + 
                 rales + elevjvp + pedema + restdys, 
             data = dig_df,
             n.trees = 5000,
             interaction.depth = 2,
             stop.method = c("es.mean"),
             estimand = "ATT",
             verbose = FALSE)
Code 
plot(ps_dig)

We can see that the simulation has run long enough to stabilize estimates.

4.2 Assessing Balance

Code 
b2 <- bal.tab(ps_dig, full.stop.method = "es.mean.att", 
        stats = c("m", "v"), un = TRUE)

b2
Balance Measures
               Type Diff.Un V.Ratio.Un Diff.Adj V.Ratio.Adj
prop.score Distance  0.6965     1.1618   0.4275      1.2898
age         Contin. -0.0238     0.9625  -0.0106      1.0202
race_2       Binary  0.0187          .   0.0083           .
sex_2        Binary  0.0149          .   0.0087           .
bmi         Contin.  0.0155     1.0695   0.0096      1.0980
ejf_per     Contin. -0.1619     1.0072  -0.0143      1.0078
klevel      Contin. -1.8020     0.0007  -0.5671      0.0021
creat       Contin.  0.0236     1.0474   0.0134      1.0456
sysbp       Contin. -0.0038     1.0329   0.0081      1.0504
diabp       Contin. -0.0057     0.9337   0.0007      1.0128
heartrte    Contin. -0.0170     0.9878   0.0001      1.0457
chfdur      Contin. -0.0249     0.9016   0.0128      1.0355
diabetes     Binary -0.0266          .  -0.0062           .
hyperten     Binary  0.0067          .   0.0065           .
prevmi       Binary  0.0083          .   0.0062           .
angina       Binary -0.0208          .  -0.0050           .
functcls    Contin.  0.0677     1.0503   0.0190      1.0237
rales       Contin.  0.0279     0.9866   0.0084      1.0043
elevjvp     Contin.  0.0663     1.0080   0.0184      1.0047
pedema      Contin. -0.0132     0.9860  -0.0127      0.9897
restdys     Contin.  0.0122     1.0027  -0.0025      1.0140

Effective sample sizes
           Control Treated
Unadjusted 3783.      3017
Adjusted   3474.54    3017

4.3 Rubin’s Rules After Weighting

4.3.1 Rubin’s Rule 1.

Code 
100*b2[1]$Balance$Diff.Un[1] # imbalance prior to weighting
[1] 69.6529
Code 
100*b2[1]$Balance$Diff.Adj[1] # imbalance after weighting
[1] 42.74692

This is still indicative of some difficulty reaching a completely reasonable level of balance, but improved.

4.3.2 Rubin’s Rule 2.

Code 
b2[1]$Balance$V.Ratio.Un[1] # imbalance prior to weighting
[1] 1.161813
Code 
b2[1]$Balance$V.Ratio.Adj[1] # imbalance after weighting
[1] 1.289799

There is no particular problem with Rule 2.

4.4 Love plots

4.4.1 Standardized Differences

Code 
p <- love.plot(b2, threshold = .1, size = 3, stars = "raw",
               title = "Standardized Diffs and ATT weights")
p

4.4.2 Variance Ratios

Code 
p <- love.plot(b2, stat = "v",
               threshold = 1.25, size = 3, 
               title = "Variance Ratios: TWANG ATT weighting")
p

We can see that klevel is not improved through the ATT weighting.

4.5 Propensity-weighted Estimate for the Outcome whf

Code 
digwts_design <- svydesign(
    ids=~1, weights=~get.weights(ps_dig, stop.method = "es.mean"),
    data=dig_df) # using twang ATT weights

adj_whf_wtd <- svyglm(whf ~ diguse, design = digwts_design,
                      family=quasibinomial())

wt_twangatt_res_whf <- 
    tidy(adj_whf_wtd, exponentiate = TRUE,
         conf.int = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95) |>
    select(term, estimate, std.error,
           lo95 = conf.low, hi95 = conf.high)

wt_twangatt_res_whf |> gt() |> 
  fmt_number(decimals = 3)
term estimate std.error lo95 hi95
(Intercept) 0.365 0.039 0.338 0.394
diguse 1.630 0.054 1.466 1.813

After ATT propensity weighting, patients who used Digoxin within one week prior to randomization had 1.630 times the odds of having worsening heart failure compared to those not used, with a 95% CI ranging from 1.466 to 1.813.

5 Task 5. Discusion.

A table of all of the results I have obtained is built here.

Method Est. Causal Effect Est. 95% CI
No covariate adjustment 1.741 (1.570 - 1.932)
1:1 matching 1.648 (1.474 - 1.843)
PS weighting 1.630 (1.466 - 1.813)

The estimated effects were highly consistent across the three analytic approaches. The unadjusted analysis yielded an effect estimate of 1.741 (95% CI: 1.570, 1.932), while the 1:1 matched analysis and propensity score–weighted analysis produced estimates of 1.648 (95% CI: 1.474, 1.843) and 1.630 (95% CI: 1.466, 1.813), respectively. The close agreement in both point estimates and confidence intervals suggests that the choice of method had limited impact on the estimated effect in this study.

The crude analysis suggested a strong association between Digoxin use and the outcome. After adjustment using propensity score methods, the estimated effect was weakened, indicating that part of the crude association was likely due to confounding.

Notably, the estimates obtained from matching and weighting were very similar, suggesting that the results are relatively robust to the choice of propensity score method. However, examination of covariate balance after adjustment reveals some limitations of the propensity score weighting approach. In particular, the adjusted standardized difference for potassium level (klevel), which exhibited the largest imbalance prior to adjustment, remained relatively elevated after weighting. This suggests that the weighting model did not fully achieve balance for this covariate, indicating potential residual imbalance despite the overall similarity in effect estimates.

Patients who used Digoxin prior to randomization had a higher risk of worsening heart failure during follow-up. However, this likely reflects confounding by indication, as patients receiving Digoxin before the trial were likely to have more severe underlying disease. Despite adjustment using propensity score methods, residual confounding may still remain. Digoxin use prior to randomization may serve as a marker of disease severity rather than a causal risk factor.

6 Use of AI

AI supported this project by assisting with R code development and helping refine sentence grammar.

Source Code
---
title: "500 Lab 4"
subtitle: "for PQHS/CRSP 500"
author: "Lily Zhang"
date: last-modified
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    embed-resources: true
    date-format: iso
    theme: zephyr 
---

# R Setup {.unnumbered}

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

knitr::opts_chunk$set(comment = NA) 

library(janitor) 
library(naniar)
library(conflicted)
library(tableone)
library(gt)
library(mosaic)
library(broom)
library(Epi)
library(mice)
library(Matching)
library(survival)
library(cobalt)
library(rbounds)
library(twang)
library(survey)
library(easystats)
library(tidyverse) 
conflicts_prefer(janitor::clean_names, dplyr::select, dplyr::filter, 
                 base::mean, base::max, stats::var)
theme_set(theme_bw()) 
```

## Loading the Data {.unnumbered}

```{r}
url_1 <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/THOMASELOVE/500-data/master/data/dig1.csv"

dig1_raw <- read_csv(url_1, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(subjectid = as.character(subjectid)) |>
  clean_names()

dim(dig1_raw)
```

I have 6800 rows and 72 columns here. 

The variables in the raw data are listed. 

```{r}
names(dig1_raw)
```

# Task 1. Create a Table 1

The primary outcome of this study is worsening heart failure (`whf`). The main exposure of interest is whether the patient used DIG within one week prior to randomization (`diguse`), coded as a binary indicator. All variables included in the analysis are summarized below.

Variable | Description
-------: | -----------------------------------------------------
`whf` | Worsening heart failure (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`diguse` | Whether the patient used DIG within one week prior to randomization (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`age` | Age in years
`race` | White (1) or Non-White (2)
`sex` | Male (1) or Female (2)
`bmi` | Body-Mass Index ($kg/meters^2$)
`ejf_per` | Ejection Fraction (percent)
`creat` | Serum Creatinine level (mg/dl)
`klevel` | Serum Potassium level (mEq/l)
`sysbp` | Sysolic BP (mm Hg)
`diabp` | Diastolic BP (mm Hg)
`heartrte` | Heart Rate (beats/min)
`diabetes` | History of Diabetes  (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`hyperten` | History of Hypertension  (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`prevmi` | Previous Myocardial Infarction (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`angina` | Current Angina (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`functcls` | Current NYHA Functional Class (1 = I, 2 = II, 3 = III, 4 = IV)

## Selecting Variables

First, I will select only those variables I will actually use in our study, which includes the subject ID, the PREVMI information, and the 12 variables listed above.

```{r}
dig <- dig1_raw |> 
  select(subjectid, whf, diguse, age, race, sex, bmi, 
         ejf_per, klevel, creat, sysbp, diabp, heartrte, 
         diabetes, hyperten, prevmi, angina, functcls, 
         chfdur, rales, elevjvp, pedema, restdys)

dim(dig)
```

I now have 6800 observations on 18 variables in the `dig` tibble.

## Converting Categorical Variables to Factors

First, a new variable `event` was created as a factor derived from the outcome `whf`, with levels coded as "Yes" and "No".

```{r}
dig$event <- factor(dig$whf, levels = c(1, 0), 
                    labels = c("Yes", "No"))
```

The categorical variables I will want to treat using factors in R are:

Variable | Description
-------: | -----------------------------------------------------
`diguse` | Whether the patient used DIG within one week prior to randomization (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`race` | White (1) or Non-White (2)
`sex` | Male (1) or Female (2)
`diabetes` | History of Diabetes  (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`hyperten` | History of Hypertension  (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`prevmi` | Previous Myocardial Infarction (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`angina` | Current Angina (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
`functcls` | Current NYHA Functional Class (1 = I, 2 = II, 3 = III, 4 = IV) 
`chfdur` | Approximate duration of CHF (months)
`rales` | Rales (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)
`elevjvp` | Elevated jugular venous pressure (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)
`pedema` | Peripheral edema (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)
`restdys` | Dyspnea at rest or orthopnea (0 = None or Unknown, 1 = Present, 2 = Past, 3 = Present and Past)

```{r}
dig_c <- dig |>
  mutate(diguse_f = fct_recode(factor(diguse), Yes = "1", No = "0"), 
         diguse_f = fct_relevel(diguse_f, "Yes"),
         race_f = fct_recode(factor(race), White = "1", "Non-white" = "2"),
         race_f = fct_relevel(race_f, "Non-white"),
         sex_f = fct_recode(factor(sex), M = "1", F = "2"),
         sex_f = fct_relevel(sex_f, "M"),
         diab_f = fct_recode(factor(diabetes), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         diab_f = fct_relevel(diab_f, "Yes"), 
         hyper_f = fct_recode(factor(hyperten), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         hyper_f = fct_relevel(hyper_f, "Yes"), 
         prevmi_f = fct_recode(factor(prevmi), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         prevmi_f = fct_relevel(prevmi_f, "Yes"), 
         angina_f = fct_recode(factor(angina), Yes = "1", No = "0"),
         angina_f = fct_relevel(angina_f, "Yes"), 
         nyha_f = fct_recode(factor(functcls), I = "1", II = "2",
                             III = "3", IV = "4"), 
         rales_f = factor(rales), 
         elevjvp_f = factor(elevjvp), 
         pedema_f = factor(pedema), 
         restdys_f = factor(restdys)) |> 
  select(subjectid, whf, event, diguse, diguse_f, age, race, race_f, 
         sex, sex_f, bmi, ejf_per, klevel, creat, sysbp, diabp, heartrte, 
         diabetes, diab_f, hyperten, hyper_f, prevmi, prevmi_f, 
         angina, angina_f, functcls, nyha_f, chfdur, rales, rales_f, 
         elevjvp, elevjvp_f, pedema, pedema_f, restdys, restdys_f)

dim(dig_c)
```

Now, the variables are properly formatted for analysis. 

## Numeric Summaries for Continuous Variables

```{r}
dig_c |> 
  select(age, bmi, ejf_per, klevel, creat, sysbp, diabp, heartrte, chfdur) |> 
  summary()
```

Ranges of the variables look reasonable. 

## Data Set After Cleaning

Missingness is examined for all variables.

```{r}
miss_var_summary(dig_c)
miss_case_table(dig_c) 
```

I do not have any missing values on the outcome `whf`. 

## Codebook for Analytic Data

```{r}
data_codebook(dig_c |> select(-subjectid))
```

## Resulting Table 1

```{r}
table1 <- CreateTableOne(data = dig_c, 
          vars = c("whf", "age", "race_f", "sex_f", "bmi", 
                   "ejf_per", "klevel", "creat", "sysbp", 
                   "diabp", "heartrte", "chfdur", "diab_f", 
                   "hyper_f", "prevmi_f", "angina_f", "nyha_f", 
                   "rales_f", "elevjvp_f", "pedema_f","restdys_f"),
          strata = c("diguse_f"))
table1
```

There is not much difference between the two treatment groups across all covariates. 

# Task 2. Unadjusted Analysis.

An unadjusted model (`m1`) was constructed to evaluate the effect of treatment  on the outcome `death`.

```{r}
temp <- twoby2(table(dig_c$diguse_f, dig_c$event))
```

```{r}
unadj <- glm(whf ~ diguse, data=dig_c, family=binomial())

model_parameters(unadj, exponentiate = TRUE, ci = 0.95) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(columns = -df_error, decimals = 3) 
```

Patients who used Digoxin within one week prior to randomization had 1.741 times higher odds of worsening heart failure compared to those not used (OR = 1.741, 95% CI: 1.570 - 1.932), suggesting a meaningful difference between the two groups.

# Task 3. 1:1 Matching.

## Single Imputation

There is some missingness in the data set. To appropriately handle missing data, single imputation is performed prior to fitting the propensity score model.

```{r}
dig_i <- 
  mice(dig_c, m = 1, seed = 500, print = FALSE) |>
  complete() |>
  tibble()
```

## PS Model

A propensity score model was fitted using logistic regression to estimate the probability of treatment assignment conditional on observed covariates. 

```{r}
psmodel <- glm(diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + ejf_per + klevel + 
                 creat + sysbp + diabp + heartrte + chfdur + 
                 diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + angina + functcls + 
                 rales + elevjvp + pedema + restdys, 
               family=binomial(), data=dig_i)

summary(psmodel)
```

Having fit the model, the raw and linear propensity score values are saved to the data set. 

```{r}
dig_i$ps <- psmodel$fitted
dig_i$linps <- psmodel$linear.predictors
```

## Comparing Propensity Score Distribution

A numeric summary of the PS is printed:

```{r}
df_stats(ps + linps ~ diguse, data = dig_i) |>
  gt() |> fmt_number(columns = c(-n, -missing), decimals = 3) |> 
  opt_stylize(style = 2, color = "cyan")
```

The distribution of PS across the two treatment groups are compared. 

```{r}
ggplot(dig_i, aes(x = diguse_f, y = ps, color = diguse_f)) + 
    geom_boxplot() +
    geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.5) + 
    guides(color = "none")
```

```{r}
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5
ggplot(dig_i, aes(x = linps, fill = diguse_f)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.3)
```

We can see a fair amount of overlap across the two treatment groups. I will use Rubin’s Rules to help assess the amount of overlap at this point, before any adjustments for the propensity score.

## Rubin’s Rules

### Rubin’s Rule 1

First, the absolute value of the standardized difference of the linear propensity score, comparing the intervention group to the control group, should be close to 0, ideally below 10%, and in any case less than 50%. 

```{r}
rubin1.unadj <- with(dig_i,
     abs(100*(mean(linps[diguse==1])-mean(linps[diguse==0]))/sd(linps)))
rubin1.unadj
```

Rubin's Rule 1 is barely satisfied. 

### Rubin’s Rule 2

Second, the ratio of the variance of the linear propensity score in the intervention group to the variance of the linear propensity score in the control group should be close to 1, ideally between 4/5 and 5/4, but certainly between 1/2 and 2.

```{r}
rubin2.unadj <- with(dig_i,
  var(linps[diguse == 1]) / var(linps[diguse == 0]))

rubin2.unadj
```

Rubin’s Rule 2 is not satisfied, further adjustment of the propensity score is needed to achieve better covariate balance. Therefore, a 1:1 greedy matching procedure will be performed.

## 1:1 Matching

### Matching without Replacement

To match on the linear propensity score: 

```{r}
X <- dig_i$linps 
Tr <- as.logical(dig_i$diguse)
match1 <- Match(Tr=Tr, X=X, M = 1, replace=FALSE, ties=FALSE)
summary(match1)
```

### Assessing the Balance

Below I will assess the match balance from the 1:1 matching.

```{r}
set.seed(500)
mb1 <- MatchBalance(diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + 
                    ejf_per + klevel + creat + sysbp + diabp + 
                    heartrte + chfdur + diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + 
                    angina + functcls + rales + elevjvp + pedema + 
                    restdys + ps + linps, 
                    data=dig_i, match.out = match1, nboots=500)
```

```{r}
covnames <- c("age", "race", "sex", "bmi", 
              "ejf_per", "klevel", "creat", "sysbp", "diabp", 
              "heartrte", "chfdur", "diabetes", "hyperten", "prevmi",
              "angina", "functcls", "rales", "elevjvp", "pedema", 
              "restdys", "ps", "linps")

pre.szd <- NULL; post.szd <- NULL
for(i in 1:length(covnames)) {
pre.szd[i] <- mb1$BeforeMatching[[i]]$sdiff.pooled
post.szd[i] <- mb1$AfterMatching[[i]]$sdiff.pooled
}
```

```{r}
match_szd <- data.frame(covnames, pre.szd, post.szd)
print(match_szd, digits=3)
```

Next, a Love Plot is used to describing standardized differences before and after matching

```{r}
b <- bal.tab(match1, 
             diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + ejf_per + klevel + 
                 creat + sysbp + diabp + heartrte + chfdur + 
                 diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + angina + functcls + 
                 rales + elevjvp + pedema + restdys + ps + linps, 
             data=dig_i, stats = c("m", "v"), un = TRUE,
             quick = FALSE)
```

```{r}
p <- love.plot(b, threshold = .1, size = 3,
               var.order = "unadjusted", stars = "raw",
               title = "Standardized Differences and 1:1 Matching")
p
```

Variance ratios before and after matching are also illustrated. 

```{r}
p <- love.plot(b, stat = "v",
               threshold = 1.25, size = 3,
               var.order = "unadjusted",
               title = "Variance Ratios and 1:1 Matching")
p + theme_bw()
```

We can see that the balance of all covariates has improved after adjustment. 

### Rubin’s Rules 1 and 2 for Matching 

Create a new data frame, containing only the matched sample.

```{r}
matches_1 <- factor(rep(match1$index.treated, 2))

dig.matches_1 <- cbind(matches_1, 
          dig_i[c(match1$index.control, match1$index.treated),])
```

Rubin’s Rules 1: 

```{r}
rubin1.m1 <- with(dig.matches_1, 
      abs(100*(mean(linps[diguse==1])-mean(linps[diguse==0]))/sd(linps)))                         
rubin1.m1
```

Rubin’s Rules 2: 

```{r}
rubin2.m1 <- with(dig.matches_1, 
                  var(linps[diguse == 1]) / var(linps[diguse == 0]))
rubin2.m1
```

Rubin’s Rules 1 and 2 are both satisfied. 

## Causal Effect Estimate

```{r}
whf_adj_m1 <- clogit(whf ~ diguse + strata(matches_1), data = dig.matches_1)

summary(whf_adj_m1)
```

```{r}
tidy(whf_adj_m1, exponentiate = TRUE, 
     conf.int = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95) |> 
  gt() |> fmt_number(decimals = 3)
```

After 1:1 propensity score matching, patients who used Digoxin within one week prior to randomization had 1.648 times the odds of having worsening heart failure compared to those not used, with a 95% CI ranging from 1.474 to 1.843, suggesting that there are meaningful difference between two groups. 

## Sensitivity Analyses After Matching

A Rosenbaum sensitivity analysis was conducted to assess the robustness of the estimated treatment effect to potential unmeasured confounding.

```{r}
pair_data <- dig.matches_1 |>
  group_by(matches_1) |>
  summarise(y_treat = whf[diguse == 1],  
            y_ctrl  = whf[diguse == 0], 
            .groups = "drop")

out.tab <- table(
  No_diguseazine = pair_data$y_ctrl,
  diguseazine = pair_data$y_treat)

out.tab
```

```{r}
binarysens(x = out.tab["1", "0"], y = out.tab["0", "1"], 
           Gamma = 2, GammaInc = 0.05)
```

The treatment effect remained robust to unmeasured confounding up to $\Gamma = 1.65$. Beyond this level, the observed effect could be explained away by potential hidden bias. This suggests that the results are moderately sensitive to unmeasured confounding. 

# Task 4. Propensity Weighting Analysis.

## Propensity Score Weighting (ATT approach)

```{r}
dig_df <- data.frame(dig_i)
```

```{r}
ps_dig <- ps(diguse ~ age + race + sex + bmi + ejf_per + klevel + 
                 creat + sysbp + diabp + heartrte + chfdur + 
                 diabetes + hyperten + prevmi + angina + functcls + 
                 rales + elevjvp + pedema + restdys, 
             data = dig_df,
             n.trees = 5000,
             interaction.depth = 2,
             stop.method = c("es.mean"),
             estimand = "ATT",
             verbose = FALSE)
```

```{r}
plot(ps_dig)
```

We can see that the simulation has run long enough to stabilize estimates. 

## Assessing Balance

```{r}
b2 <- bal.tab(ps_dig, full.stop.method = "es.mean.att", 
        stats = c("m", "v"), un = TRUE)

b2
```

## Rubin’s Rules After Weighting

### Rubin’s Rule 1.

```{r}
100*b2[1]$Balance$Diff.Un[1] # imbalance prior to weighting
```

```{r}
100*b2[1]$Balance$Diff.Adj[1] # imbalance after weighting
```

This is still indicative of some difficulty reaching a completely reasonable level of balance, but improved. 

### Rubin’s Rule 2.

```{r}
b2[1]$Balance$V.Ratio.Un[1] # imbalance prior to weighting
```

```{r}
b2[1]$Balance$V.Ratio.Adj[1] # imbalance after weighting
```

There is no particular problem with Rule 2.

## Love plots

### Standardized Differences

```{r}
p <- love.plot(b2, threshold = .1, size = 3, stars = "raw",
               title = "Standardized Diffs and ATT weights")
p
```

### Variance Ratios

```{r}
p <- love.plot(b2, stat = "v",
               threshold = 1.25, size = 3, 
               title = "Variance Ratios: TWANG ATT weighting")
p
```

We can see that `klevel` is not improved through the ATT weighting. 

## Propensity-weighted Estimate for the Outcome `whf`

```{r}
digwts_design <- svydesign(
    ids=~1, weights=~get.weights(ps_dig, stop.method = "es.mean"),
    data=dig_df) # using twang ATT weights

adj_whf_wtd <- svyglm(whf ~ diguse, design = digwts_design,
                      family=quasibinomial())

wt_twangatt_res_whf <- 
    tidy(adj_whf_wtd, exponentiate = TRUE,
         conf.int = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95) |>
    select(term, estimate, std.error,
           lo95 = conf.low, hi95 = conf.high)

wt_twangatt_res_whf |> gt() |> 
  fmt_number(decimals = 3)
```

After ATT propensity weighting, patients who used Digoxin within one week prior to randomization had 1.630 times the odds of having worsening heart failure compared to those not used, with a 95% CI ranging from 1.466 to 1.813. 

# Task 5. Discusion.

A table of all of the results I have obtained is built here. 

Method | Est. Causal Effect | Est. 95% CI | 
--------------: | :-----------: | :-----------:  
No covariate adjustment | 1.741 | (1.570 - 1.932) 
1:1 matching | 1.648 | (1.474 - 1.843) 
PS weighting | 1.630 | (1.466 - 1.813)

The estimated effects were highly consistent across the three analytic approaches. The unadjusted analysis yielded an effect estimate of 1.741 (95% CI: 1.570, 1.932), while the 1:1 matched analysis and propensity score–weighted analysis produced estimates of 1.648 (95% CI: 1.474, 1.843) and 1.630 (95% CI: 1.466, 1.813), respectively. The close agreement in both point estimates and confidence intervals suggests that the choice of method had limited impact on the estimated effect in this study. 

The crude analysis suggested a strong association between Digoxin use and the outcome. After adjustment using propensity score methods, the estimated effect was weakened, indicating that part of the crude association was likely due to confounding. 

Notably, the estimates obtained from matching and weighting were very similar, suggesting that the results are relatively robust to the choice of propensity score method. However, examination of covariate balance after adjustment reveals some limitations of the propensity score weighting approach. In particular, the adjusted standardized difference for potassium level (klevel), which exhibited the largest imbalance prior to adjustment, remained relatively elevated after weighting. This suggests that the weighting model did not fully achieve balance for this covariate, indicating potential residual imbalance despite the overall similarity in effect estimates. 

Patients who used Digoxin prior to randomization had a higher risk of worsening heart failure during follow-up. However, this likely reflects confounding by indication, as patients receiving Digoxin before the trial were likely to have more severe underlying disease. Despite adjustment using propensity score methods, residual confounding may still remain. Digoxin use prior to randomization may serve as a marker of disease severity rather than a causal risk factor.

# Use of AI

AI supported this project by assisting with R code development and helping refine sentence grammar.

# Session Information

```{r}
xfun::session_info()
```
